title: Depression — Major
oneLiner: Common mood disorder with persistent low mood and anhedonia; affects 1 in 6; high suicide risk if untreated.

sections.Major Depressive Disorder (Depression —: **One-liner:** Common mood disorder with persistent low mood and anhedonia; affects 1 in 6; high suicide risk if untreated.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Suicide attempt (imminent)** – ask: “Do you have thoughts of harming yourself?” If yes → plan/intent/means → **emergency psychiatry**, 1:1 observation, remove belts/shoelaces/sharps, secure room.
- **Malignant catatonia** – fever, autonomic instability (BP/heart rate swings), rigidity, mutism. Single finding: **waxy flexibility** + hyperthermia. → Urgent lorazepam 1–2 mg IM/IV [dose per local policy]; if no response, call psychiatry reg.
- **Serotonin syndrome / NMS** – on SSRIs/SNRIs/MAOIs or antipsychotics. Look for **clonus**, **hyperthermia**, **agitation**, **tachycardia**. → Stop all serotonergic/antipsychotic drugs, supportive care, consider cyproheptadine/give dantrolene [per local critical care protocol]; call med reg.
sections.Presentation:
- Core (≥2 weeks): depressed mood most of day, anhedonia, fatigue/low energy.
- Sleep disturbance (insomnia or hypersomnia), appetite change (↓ or ↑), poor concentration, indecisiveness, feelings of worthlessness/guilt, recurrent thoughts of death/suicide.
- Exam: psychomotor agitation or retardation, flat affect, poor eye contact, weight loss/gain, self-harm scars, somatic complaints (pain, headache, GI upset).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Bipolar disorder (current depressive episode)** – prior mania/hypomania (“decreased need for sleep”, “unusually happy/irritable”, “risky behaviour”). *Splitter:* Ask about hypomanic episodes – if yes, bipolar not unipolar.
- **Adjustment disorder** – symptoms related to identifiable stressor, less severe, duration <6 months after stressor resolution.
- **Anxiety disorders (GAD, panic)** – predominant worry/panic/avoidance, not persistent low mood.
- **Dementia (pseudodementia)** – elderly with cognitive decline; differentiate by rapid onset, mood symptoms before cognitive loss, “I don’t know” answers vs effortful attempts.
- **Organic causes** – hypothyroidism (check TSH), B12/folate deficiency, anaemia, drugs (beta-blockers, steroids, IFN), chronic illness (cancer, CKD).
- **Bereavement** – normal grief; symptoms wave-like, focused on loss, self-esteem preserved. If >2 months with severe functional impairment → depression.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** PHQ-9 score (0–27; ≥10 moderate, ≥20 severe); suicidal ideation assessment (direct question); collateral from family; cognitive screen (MMSE) if older.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&Es, LFTs, TFTs, vitamin B12/folate, glucose; consider drug screen.
- **Imaging:** Not routine. If new-onset severe depression in elderly or focal neurology/cognitive decline → CT head.
- **Special tests:** ECG if starting citalopram/escitalopram (QTc risk) or if electrolyte abnormality; baseline weight/BMI.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** ICD-10 or DSM-5. ICD-10: core symptoms (depressed mood, loss of interest, low energy) + other symptoms. Mild: 2 core + 2 others; moderate: 2 core + 3–4; severe: 3 core + ≥4 others ± psychotic features.
- **Never-miss test:** Suicide risk assessment – asking “do you have thoughts of ending your life?”. Omission is lethal.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** If suicidal crisis → 1:1 observation, remove means, contact liaison psychiatry. If catatonia → lorazepam (dose per local policy). If serotonin syndrome/NMS → stop drugs, supportive care, ICU referral if unstable.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):** Start SSRI if moderate–severe depression with no contraindications. Sertraline 50 mg once daily (titrate to 200 mg at 2-weekly intervals). Citalopram 20 mg once daily (max 40 mg; 20 mg if >65 y, hepatic impairment, QTc risk). Mirtazapine 15 mg once daily (sedating, use if insomnia/weight loss). Monitor for side effects (nausea, headache, anxiety). Provide psychoeducation – antidepressant response 2–4 weeks. Refer to liaison psychiatry if psychotic features, severe impairment, or diagnostic uncertainty.
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Continue antidepressant for ≥6 months after remission (first episode); ≥2 years if recurrent. Refer for psychological therapy (CBT, IPT). Lifestyle: sleep hygiene, exercise, social support. Crisis plan with crisis team number.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** No special fluids/obs unless immobile. VTE prophylaxis if immobility (e.g., catatonia) according to local policy. Diet: normal; encourage high-calorie supplements if poor intake. Observations: if on suicide precautions – 1:1 documented; NEWS2 q4h if on medications affecting vitals.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- First-line: **Sertraline** 50 mg PO once daily (increase to 200 mg as tolerated). **Citalopram** 20 mg PO once daily (max 40 mg; 20 mg in >65 y or hepatic impairment). **Escitalopram** 10 mg PO once daily (max 20 mg). **Fluoxetine** 20 mg PO once daily (long half-life, suitable if compliance concern).
- Common alternatives: **Mirtazapine** 15–45 mg PO at night (sedating, good for insomnia/weight loss). **Venlafaxine** (SNRI) 37.5–75 mg PO BD or modified-release 75 mg once daily, titrate to 225 mg daily; monitor BP. **Amitriptyline** (TCA) 25–75 mg PO at night (start low, max 150 mg; avoid in elderly due to anticholinergic/overdose risk).
- Key contraindications: MAOIs (including linezolid) – avoid 14 days after SSRI. Citalopram/escitalopram – avoid if QTc >500 ms. Mirtazapine – caution in hepatic impairment.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Sertraline – no dose adjustment; caution in severe hepatic impairment. Citalopram – max 20 mg in hepatic impairment. Most SSRIs safe in renal impairment; check BNF.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Starting SSRI at too high dose (e.g., sertraline 100 mg initially → severe nausea/anxiety). Also: **abrupt withdrawal** of SSRI/SNRI → discontinuation syndrome (dizziness, paraesthesias, flu-like). Taper over ≥4 weeks. TCA overdose (e.g., amitriptyline) is lethal – avoid in suicidal patients.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Suicide attempt, serotonin syndrome (especially with combination of serotonergic drugs), catatonia, psychosis (mood-congruent delusions), worsening agitation/akathisia in first 2 weeks of SSRI (especially <25 y).
- **Chronic:** Functional impairment, social withdrawal, cognitive deficits, increased cardiovascular morbidity, substance misuse, treatment-resistant depression.
sections.Prognosis: - 60–70% respond to first-line SSRI. Recurrence: ~50% after first episode, >90% after 3 episodes. Chronic depression (>2 years) occurs in ~20%. Mortality increased from suicide (lifetime risk ~4%) and cardiovascular disease.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- Always ask about suicidal ideation directly – it does NOT increase risk; it builds rapport and saves lives.
- Monitor closely in first 2 weeks of SSRI – warn about transient worsening of anxiety/agitation; this is normal.
- Check TSH and B12/folate before labelling as depression – treating underlying cause can resolve symptoms.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- Do not prescribe benzodiazepines alone for depression – they do not treat core mood and risk dependence.
- Do not rely solely on PHQ-9 – clinical judgement of severity and suicide risk is essential.
- Do not stop antidepressants abruptly – always plan a 4-week taper to avoid discontinuation syndrome.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Suicidal thoughts worsen or plan develops; new agitation/confusion/chest pain; severe side effects (e.g., rash, swelling); no improvement after 4 weeks.
- **Follow-up:** GP or psychiatric outpatient within 2 weeks. Provide crisis team / Samaritans number. Consider contact with community mental health team.
sections.Escalation Criteria: - NEWS2: not typically unstable, but if serotonin syndrome / NMS → lactate >2, temperature >38.5°C, GCS <15, HR >120, BP <90/60 →

rawMarkdown:
## Major Depressive Disorder (Depression — Major)
**One-liner:** Common mood disorder with persistent low mood and anhedonia; affects 1 in 6; high suicide risk if untreated.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Suicide attempt (imminent)** – ask: “Do you have thoughts of harming yourself?” If yes → plan/intent/means → **emergency psychiatry**, 1:1 observation, remove belts/shoelaces/sharps, secure room.
- **Malignant catatonia** – fever, autonomic instability (BP/heart rate swings), rigidity, mutism. Single finding: **waxy flexibility** + hyperthermia. → Urgent lorazepam 1–2 mg IM/IV [dose per local policy]; if no response, call psychiatry reg.
- **Serotonin syndrome / NMS** – on SSRIs/SNRIs/MAOIs or antipsychotics. Look for **clonus**, **hyperthermia**, **agitation**, **tachycardia**. → Stop all serotonergic/antipsychotic drugs, supportive care, consider cyproheptadine/give dantrolene [per local critical care protocol]; call med reg.

### Presentation
- Core (≥2 weeks): depressed mood most of day, anhedonia, fatigue/low energy.
- Sleep disturbance (insomnia or hypersomnia), appetite change (↓ or ↑), poor concentration, indecisiveness, feelings of worthlessness/guilt, recurrent thoughts of death/suicide.
- Exam: psychomotor agitation or retardation, flat affect, poor eye contact, weight loss/gain, self-harm scars, somatic complaints (pain, headache, GI upset).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Bipolar disorder (current depressive episode)** – prior mania/hypomania (“decreased need for sleep”, “unusually happy/irritable”, “risky behaviour”). *Splitter:* Ask about hypomanic episodes – if yes, bipolar not unipolar.
- **Adjustment disorder** – symptoms related to identifiable stressor, less severe, duration <6 months after stressor resolution.
- **Anxiety disorders (GAD, panic)** – predominant worry/panic/avoidance, not persistent low mood.
- **Dementia (pseudodementia)** – elderly with cognitive decline; differentiate by rapid onset, mood symptoms before cognitive loss, “I don’t know” answers vs effortful attempts.
- **Organic causes** – hypothyroidism (check TSH), B12/folate deficiency, anaemia, drugs (beta-blockers, steroids, IFN), chronic illness (cancer, CKD).
- **Bereavement** – normal grief; symptoms wave-like, focused on loss, self-esteem preserved. If >2 months with severe functional impairment → depression.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** PHQ-9 score (0–27; ≥10 moderate, ≥20 severe); suicidal ideation assessment (direct question); collateral from family; cognitive screen (MMSE) if older.
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&Es, LFTs, TFTs, vitamin B12/folate, glucose; consider drug screen.
- **Imaging:** Not routine. If new-onset severe depression in elderly or focal neurology/cognitive decline → CT head.
- **Special tests:** ECG if starting citalopram/escitalopram (QTc risk) or if electrolyte abnormality; baseline weight/BMI.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** ICD-10 or DSM-5. ICD-10: core symptoms (depressed mood, loss of interest, low energy) + other symptoms. Mild: 2 core + 2 others; moderate: 2 core + 3–4; severe: 3 core + ≥4 others ± psychotic features.
- **Never-miss test:** Suicide risk assessment – asking “do you have thoughts of ending your life?”. Omission is lethal.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** If suicidal crisis → 1:1 observation, remove means, contact liaison psychiatry. If catatonia → lorazepam (dose per local policy). If serotonin syndrome/NMS → stop drugs, supportive care, ICU referral if unstable.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):** Start SSRI if moderate–severe depression with no contraindications. Sertraline 50 mg once daily (titrate to 200 mg at 2-weekly intervals). Citalopram 20 mg once daily (max 40 mg; 20 mg if >65 y, hepatic impairment, QTc risk). Mirtazapine 15 mg once daily (sedating, use if insomnia/weight loss). Monitor for side effects (nausea, headache, anxiety). Provide psychoeducation – antidepressant response 2–4 weeks. Refer to liaison psychiatry if psychotic features, severe impairment, or diagnostic uncertainty.
- **Long-term / outpatient:** Continue antidepressant for ≥6 months after remission (first episode); ≥2 years if recurrent. Refer for psychological therapy (CBT, IPT). Lifestyle: sleep hygiene, exercise, social support. Crisis plan with crisis team number.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** No special fluids/obs unless immobile. VTE prophylaxis if immobility (e.g., catatonia) according to local policy. Diet: normal; encourage high-calorie supplements if poor intake. Observations: if on suicide precautions – 1:1 documented; NEWS2 q4h if on medications affecting vitals.

### Drugs & Doses
- First-line: **Sertraline** 50 mg PO once daily (increase to 200 mg as tolerated). **Citalopram** 20 mg PO once daily (max 40 mg; 20 mg in >65 y or hepatic impairment). **Escitalopram** 10 mg PO once daily (max 20 mg). **Fluoxetine** 20 mg PO once daily (long half-life, suitable if compliance concern).
- Common alternatives: **Mirtazapine** 15–45 mg PO at night (sedating, good for insomnia/weight loss). **Venlafaxine** (SNRI) 37.5–75 mg PO BD or modified-release 75 mg once daily, titrate to 225 mg daily; monitor BP. **Amitriptyline** (TCA) 25–75 mg PO at night (start low, max 150 mg; avoid in elderly due to anticholinergic/overdose risk).
- Key contraindications: MAOIs (including linezolid) – avoid 14 days after SSRI. Citalopram/escitalopram – avoid if QTc >500 ms. Mirtazapine – caution in hepatic impairment.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Sertraline – no dose adjustment; caution in severe hepatic impairment. Citalopram – max 20 mg in hepatic impairment. Most SSRIs safe in renal impairment; check BNF.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Starting SSRI at too high dose (e.g., sertraline 100 mg initially → severe nausea/anxiety). Also: **abrupt withdrawal** of SSRI/SNRI → discontinuation syndrome (dizziness, paraesthesias, flu-like). Taper over ≥4 weeks. TCA overdose (e.g., amitriptyline) is lethal – avoid in suicidal patients.

### Complications
- **Acute:** Suicide attempt, serotonin syndrome (especially with combination of serotonergic drugs), catatonia, psychosis (mood-congruent delusions), worsening agitation/akathisia in first 2 weeks of SSRI (especially <25 y).
- **Chronic:** Functional impairment, social withdrawal, cognitive deficits, increased cardiovascular morbidity, substance misuse, treatment-resistant depression.

### Prognosis
- 60–70% respond to first-line SSRI. Recurrence: ~50% after first episode, >90% after 3 episodes. Chronic depression (>2 years) occurs in ~20%. Mortality increased from suicide (lifetime risk ~4%) and cardiovascular disease.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- Always ask about suicidal ideation directly – it does NOT increase risk; it builds rapport and saves lives.
- Monitor closely in first 2 weeks of SSRI – warn about transient worsening of anxiety/agitation; this is normal.
- Check TSH and B12/folate before labelling as depression – treating underlying cause can resolve symptoms.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- Do not prescribe benzodiazepines alone for depression – they do not treat core mood and risk dependence.
- Do not rely solely on PHQ-9 – clinical judgement of severity and suicide risk is essential.
- Do not stop antidepressants abruptly – always plan a 4-week taper to avoid discontinuation syndrome.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Suicidal thoughts worsen or plan develops; new agitation/confusion/chest pain; severe side effects (e.g., rash, swelling); no improvement after 4 weeks.
- **Follow-up:** GP or psychiatric outpatient within 2 weeks. Provide crisis team / Samaritans number. Consider contact with community mental health team.

### Escalation Criteria
- NEWS2: not typically unstable, but if serotonin syndrome / NMS → lactate >2, temperature >38.5°C, GCS <15, HR >120, BP <90/60 →
